title: The Two-Pillar Policy for the RMB
abstract: This paper studies China's recent exchange rate policy for the renminbi (RMB). We demonstrate empirically that a two-pillar policy is in place, aiming to balance exchange rate flexibility and RMB index stability viamarketandbasketpillars. We further extend and validate the formulation that incorporates the so-called countercyclical factor. Theoretically, we develop a flexible-price monetary model for the RMB in which the two-pillar policy arises endogenously as an optimal response of the government. We estimate the model by generalized method of moments and quantitatively assess various policy trade-offs.

article.introduction:
How China manages its currency, the renminbi (RMB), is among the most consequential decisions in global financial markets. China's economy, second only to that of the United States, has grown miraculously over the last 40 years at an average rate of more than 10% annually (Song, Storesletten, and Zilibotti (2011)). After joining the World Trade Organization (WTO) in 2001, China became the world's largest exporter in 2009. Thanks to its sizable current account surplus, which reached 10% of its GDP in 2007, China's foreign reserves swelled to almost 4 trillion U.S. dollars in mid-2014. The value of the RMB is thus of paramount importance in determining the competitiveness of China's exports. Many people have argued that China's undervalued currency contributes to the trade surplus that China has run consistently since 1993.1It is therefore important to understand how China's monetary authority conducts its exchange rate policy.
China's exchange rate policy has been evolving over time, mirroring its transition from a planned economy to a market economy.2The value of the RMB was pegged to the U.S. dollar for over a decade until July 21, 2005, when the hard peg was replaced by a managed floating regime. According to the People's Bank of China (PBC), “the overall goal of reforming RMB exchange rate regime is to establish and improve a managed floating regime based on market supply and demand, and keep RMB exchange rate basically stable at an adaptive and equilibrium level.”3Under the current regime, the PBC announces thecentral parity(orfixing) rate of the RMB against the U.S. dollar before market opening each business day. The central parity rate serves as the midpoint of the daily trading range, and the intraday spot rate is allowed to fluctuate within only a narrow band around it. For a number of years, little was revealed about how the central parity rate was determined. Since August 2015, the PBC has implemented several reforms to make the formation mechanism of the central parity rate more transparent and market-oriented. Despite its improved transparency, however, China's recent exchange rate policy has been largely underresearched. In this paper, we fill this gap by empirically and theoretically examining China's exchange rate policy since 2015.
We first document novel stylized facts and show empirically that a two-pillar policy has been in place, aiming to balance exchange rateflexibilityand RMB indexstability. According to the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2016Q1), the formation mechanism of the central parity rate depends on two key factors, or pillars: the first pillar, “the market pillar,” refers to “the closing rates of the previous business day to reflect changes in market demand and supply conditions,” while the second pillar, “the basket pillar,” refers to changes in the currency basket designed “to maintain the overall stability of the RMB to the currency basket.”
The market pillar can easily be measured as the previous closing rate of the RMB against the U.S. dollar. In contrast, measuring the basket pillar is not as obvious. Moreover, the weights assigned to the two pillars are not observable. In this paper, we rigorously define and measure the basket pillar at a daily frequency. We show that our empirical measures of these two pillars explain as much as 80% of the variation in the central parity rate. We further show that both pillars receive roughly equal weight in setting the central parity rate. To examine time variation in the pillar weights, we run 60- and 90-day rolling-window regressions. The results suggest that the weights hover around 50% over time and can shift toward the basket pillar during times of market stress such as the U.S.-China trade war. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first to rigorously formulate the two-pillar policy and empirically validate that such policy is indeed implemented in practice.
We fine-tune our two-pillar policy formulation to account for two reforms in the central parity formation mechanism. Initially, the basket pillar on a given day was defined over a 24-hour reference period starting at 4:30PM the previous day. The first reform occurred on February 20, 2017, when the PBC reduced the 24-hour window to the 15-hour overnight window, which continues to start at 4:30PM the previous day but now ends at 7:30AM the following day. The second reform was the introduction of the so-calledcountercyclical factorin May 2017 to dampen depreciation pressure and stabilize the currency. However, little is known about how the countercyclical factor works. For many market participants, it is a secret “X factor.”4
As another contribution of this paper, we demystify the workings of the countercyclical factor. We show that in the presence of the countercyclical factor, the central parity rule can be well formulated by a variant of the two-pillar policy. More specifically, the basket pillar over the 24-hour reference period can be decomposed into two components: one defined over the daytime 9-hour reference period—the “daytime component” of the basket pillar—and the other defined over the overnight 15-hour reference period—the “nighttime component” of the basket pillar. We then rigorously define the countercyclical factor as an adjustment factor that removes “the impact of the currency basket from the movement between the previous closing rates and the central parity,” as stated in the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2017Q2). This factor is used to partially offset the deviation of the previous closing rate from the daytime component of the basket pillar, which may be driven by sentiment-induced “procyclicality” in the foreign exchange market according to the PBC. As a result, imposing the countercyclical factor is equivalent to shifting away from the market pillar toward the daytime component of the basket pillar, consistent with the PBC's statement that “after the introduction of the counter-cyclical factor the central parity formation mechanism has increased the weight of the reference to the currency basket.” The magnitude of the shift is determined by acountercyclical coefficient, which is set by a group of quoting banks and unknown to the public. Importantly, even in the presence of the countercyclical factor, the central parity rule is still prescribed by the (extended) two-pillar policy that depends on the market pillar and both components of the basket pillar.
We test our extended two-pillar formulation in the data and estimate the unknown countercyclical coefficient. We find that our two-pillar formulation with the countercyclical factor performs well empirically. For example, in the latest subperiod during the U.S.-China trade war when the countercyclical factor was imposed, it explains about 77% of the variation in the central parity rate. Dropping the countercyclical factor reduces the explanatory power to 70%. As a falsification test, we show that our empirical measure of the countercyclical factor has little to zero explanatory power in the subperiods in which it was not imposed. Our empirical regression results also provide us with an estimate of the unknown countercyclical coefficient of around 20%. Put differently, the market pillar, which used to take 50% of the overall weight, receives a reduced weight of 30% upon the introduction of the countercyclical factor.
To better understand China's recent exchange rate policy, we develop a flexible-price monetary model of the RMB by extending Svensson (1994). In the model, the government faces trade-offs between various policy targets. Among others, the key trade-off is that between exchange rate flexibility and current account stability. The government chooses the money growth rate and exchange rate policy to optimally balance various policy targets.
In the theoretical model, the two-pillar policy arises endogenously as an optimal solution to the government's problem. The central parity rate depends on both pillars because the government's preferences involve two policy targets: making the exchange rate more market-driven and stabilizing the current account. The former policy target incentivizes the government to set the central parity rate as close as possible to the previous closing rate, which reflects market conditions. The latter policy target requires a stable RMB index, which measures the value of the RMB against a basket of currencies of China's trading partners. When the government cares equally about both policy targets, the two pillars carry equal weight in the optimal central parity rule, consistent with our empirical findings.
We estimate key model parameters governing the weights on policy targets in the government's objective function using generalized method of moments (GMMs). Based on the estimation results, we can further quantitatively assess the trade-offs that the government faces. On the one hand, if the central parity rate depended only on the market pillar, then exchange rate flexibility would improve but the current account would become less stable; indeed, trade balance growth would be more than 40% more volatile than in the data. On the other hand, if the central parity rate depended only on the basket pillar, trade balance growth would be stabilized—its volatility would be one-half the level in the data—but the RMB would be much less flexible—the volatility of the difference between the central parity rate and the market pillar increasing to as high as 12.6%, or a 60-fold increase.
Our model also sheds new light on the rationale behind the countercyclical factor. Through the lens of our model, we show that a government that cares about the stability ofchangesin the exchange rate deviation—defined as the deviation of the spot rate from the central parity rate on the same day—finds it optimal to introduce the countercyclical factor. Theoretically, we prove that for the aforementioned government, the optimal central parity rule includes an additional term that depends negatively on the exchange rate deviation of the previous day. That is, a large depreciation of the spot rate relative to the central parity yesterday would prompt the government to counteract such movement by boosting the central parity rate today, which helps stabilize the exchange rate deviation today. Heuristically, the additional adjustment term in the optimal central parity rule works to decrease the weight of the market pillar and increase the weight of the basket pillar. Consistent with the model, the volatility of changes in the exchange rate deviation is typically lower in the data during those subperiods with the countercyclical factor relative to those without the factor.
To further capture “irrational” behavior or a “herding effect” in the foreign exchange market that motivates the countercyclical factor (see the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2017Q2)), the baseline model is extended to account for such irrational behavior via noise trading (De Long, Shleifer, Summers, and Waldmann (1990), Jeanne and Rose (2002)). Similar to Brunnermeier, Sockin, and Xiong (2020), in the extended model, exchange rate volatility explodes and the market breaks down when the amount of noise trading is sufficiently large. In this regard, the countercyclical factor is important to mitigate the destabilizing effects of irrational noise trading. Moreover, as an important policy implication, we show that by “leaning against noise trading,” direct government intervention—complementary to the countercyclical factor—is an alternative, and perhaps more effective, way to counteract irrational factors. The direct government intervention also avoids a main drawback of using the countercyclical factor, namely, the latter approach makes the central parity formation mechanism less market oriented and may hurt the government's credibility.
Our paper is related to the large literature on the Chinese exchange rate.5Earlier papers study the RMB's undervaluation or misalignment (e.g., Frankel (2007), Cheung, Chinn, and Fujii (2007), and Yu (2007)) or aim to characterize how China managed its exchange rate (e.g., Frankel and Wei (1994,2007), Frankel (2009), and Sun (2010)). In particular, Frankel and Wei (1994,2007) use regression analysis to estimateunknownbasket weights and reject the notion that an announced basket peg was followed by the PBC in earlier periods. Our empirical analysis of China's recent exchange rate policy follows and goes beyond the tradition established in Frankel and Wei (1994,2007). Recent research papers that empirically investigate the determinants of the central parity rate include Cheung, Hui, and Tsang (2018), Clark (2017), and McCauley and Shu (2018). To the best of our knowledge, our paper is the first to empirically characterize and theoretically evaluate the two-pillar policy.
Our paper is also related to the literature on exchange rate target zones initially developed for Europe's path to a monetary union (see, for example, Krugman (1991), Bertola and Caballero (1992), Bertola and Svensson (1993)). Different from the European case, China's policy features an exchange rate target that is changed daily. While Europe's objective was to move toward fixed exchange rates and a single currency, China's long-term goal is a more market-determined exchange rate.
Our flexible-price monetary model of the RMB in this paper is most closely related to the model in Svensson (1994), which is used to quantitatively analyze the degree of monetary policy independence for the managed floating system in Sweden. In Svensson (1994), the central bank preferences involve a trade-off between interest rate smoothing and exchange rate variability, and the central parity rate is assumed to be constant for the case of Sweden. By contrast, in this paper, the government optimally chooses the central parity rate. We find that the optimal central parity rule mimics the two-pillar policy for the case of China as a result of the policy trade-off between minimizing exchange rate variability and stabilizing the current account.
In the rest of the paper, SectionIcontains the empirical analysis, SectionIIpresents the theoretical analysis, and SectionIIIconcludes.
article.I. Empirical Analysis:
We start by describing official policies for the RMB in recent years. We argue that China's exchange rate policy since 2015 can be formulated by a two-pillar approach, and we provide empirical evidence for our formulation.
China's transition to a market-based economy during the last four decades has been remarkable. Like other reforms during the transition, a gradualistic approach has been followed for its exchange rate policy (Brunnermeier, Sockin, and Xiong (2017)). China began implementing a managed floating exchange rate regime on July 21, 2005, when the RMB was depegged from the dollar and observed a one-time 2% appreciation. Under the current regime, the PBC announces the central parity rate of the RMB against the U.S. dollar at 9:15AM before market opening each business day. The central parity rate serves as the midpoint of the daily trading range in the sense that the intraday spot rate is allowed to fluctuate within a narrow band around it. Panel A of Figure1displays the RMB central parity and closing rates since 2004. It is evident from the panel that the deviation of the closing rate from the central parity rate is typically very small and falls within the official trading band.
To strengthen the role of the force of demand and supply, China has gradually widened the trading band from an initial width of 0.3% to the current width of 2%.6Panel B of Figure1plots the deviation between the central parity rate and the closing rate since 2004. It shows that as the trading band widened, the deviation became more volatile, reflecting the increased flexibility of the RMB.7
On August 11, 2015, China unexpectedly reformed the central parity formation mechanism and devalued the RMB by nearly 2% against the U.S. dollar. The reformed formation mechanism is meant to be more transparent and more market driven as part of the RMB internationalization effort.8In particular, “quotes of the central parity of the RMB to the USD should refer to the closing rates of the previous business day to reflect changes in market demand and supply conditions,” according to the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2016Q1). However, the devaluation was negatively received, as market participants interpreted it as a sign of deterioration in China's economy. In response, the PBC intervened to halt further depreciation, in addition to introducing stricter capital controls and a stricter foreign exchange reserve requirement. The intervention was costly: China's foreign exchange reserves shrunk by about 1 trillion U.S. dollars to 3 trillion U.S. dollars by the end of 2016, that is, within merely 18 months.
On December 11, 2015, the PBC introduced three trade-weighted RMB indices and reformed the formation mechanism of the central parity rate. The reform aimed to mitigate depreciation expectations stemming from China's slowing economy and the first possible interest rate liftoff by the Federal Reserve. The three RMB indices are based on the China Foreign Exchange Trade System (CFETS), the IMF's Special Drawing Rights (SDR), and the Bank for International Settlement (BIS) baskets. We refer to these indices as the CFETS, SDR, and BIS indices, respectively, throughout the paper. All three indices have the same base level of 100 at the end of 2014 and are published regularly.
“a formation mechanism for the RMB to the USD central parity rate [consisting] of ‘the previous closing rate plus changes in the currency basket’ has been preliminarily in place. The ‘previous closing rate plus changes in the currency basket’ formation mechanism means that market makers must consider both factors when quoting the central parity of the RMB to the USD, namely the ‘previous closing rate’ and the ‘changes in the currency basket.'”
Intuitively, the two-pillar policy allows the PBC to make the RMB flexible and more market driven through the market pillar,StCL$S_{t}^{CL}$, and at the same time keep it stable relative to the RMB index through the basket pillar,S¯t+1$\bar{S}_{t+1}$. At one extreme, when the weightwis fixed at zero, the central parity rate is fully determined by the market pillar and thus is market driven to the extent that the spot exchange rate is permitted to fluctuate within a band around the central parity rate under possible interventions by the PBC. At the other extreme, when the weightwis fixed at 100%, the central parity rate is fully determined by the basket pillar, that is, the exchange rate policy is essentially basket pegging and the RMB index does not change over time.
The key central parity rate is that of the RMB against the U.S. dollar, denoted byStCP≡1/StCP,USD/CNY$S_{t}^{CP}\equiv 1/S_{t}^{CP,USD/CNY}$. According to the PBC, onceStCP$S_{t}^{CP}$is determined, the central parity rates for nondollar currencies are determined as the cross-rates betweenStCP$S_{t}^{CP}$and the spot exchange rates of the dollar against those currencies. Accordingly, we focus on the formation mechanism of the central parity rateStCP$ S_{t}^{CP}$, which we refer to simply as the central parity rate wherever there is no risk of confusion.
In this section, we document that the RMB central parity rate has closely tracked our equation (1) summarizing the official policy statements. In addition, we find strong empirical support for a central parity rule that gives equal weight to each of the two pillars (i.e.,w=1/2$w=1/2$).
The results from regression (7) for the full sample period are reported in Panel A of TableI. The regression results support the view thatw=1/2$w=1/2$, as the coefficients α and β are both roughly equal to one-half. The PBC's Monetary Policy Report in the first quarter of 2016 has an example that seems to suggest equal weights for both pillars. Consistent with the report, our empirical analysis provides empirical support forw=1/2$w=1/2$for the period following December 11, 2015, when the RMB indices were announced for the first time. Moreover, the regression has a very highR2of around 80%, which suggests that our formulation of the two-pillar policy has large explanatory power in describing the formation mechanism used to construct the central parity rate in practice.
The results reported in Panel A of TableIare from unconstrained regressions in which we do not impose the restriction that the coefficients sum to one. Interestingly, the regression results suggest that this restriction roughly holds in the data (i.e.,α+β≈1$\alpha +\beta \approx 1$). We also run constrained regressions in which we explicitly impose the above restriction. The results are reported in Panel B of TableI. The constrained regression results lend further support tow=1/2$w=1/2$.
The results from running the regression in equation (7) shed light on theaverageweights on the two pillars during a fixed period but are silent about possible time variation in the weights. To investigate how the weights may vary over time, we rerun the regression above using 60- and 90-day rolling windows, starting from 60 or 90 business days after August 11, 2015. Figure4plots the estimates of the weightwimplied by the rolling-window regressions.
Figure4shows that the weightwis initially around 0.1 in the period prior to the introduction of the RMB indices. The results suggest that the formation mechanism before December 2015 more closely follows a “one-pillar” policy in the sense that it is determined almost entirely by the previous day's closing rate, as the dollar basket implied by the RMB index carries very little weight.14After the RMB indices were introduced on December 11, 2015, the weightwsteadily increased to around 0.5, suggesting a two-pillar policy with equal weights. Since the introduction of a countercyclical factor in May 2017, the estimate of the weightwhas exhibited more variability, running above 0.5 mostly during the U.S.-China trade war.
The formation mechanism of the central parity rate has experienced two reforms: reduction of the reference period for the basket pillar and, more importantly, introduction of the countercyclical factor. In this subsection, we fine-tune our two-pillar policy formulation in response to these reforms. Since that little is known about the countercyclical factor, we also demystify its working mechanism. We show that in spite of the reforms, the central parity rule is still prescribed by the (extended) two-pillar policy. Furthermore, our extended two-pillar formulation with the countercyclical factor performs well empirically.
On February 20, 2017, the PBC reduced the reference period for the central parity rate against the RMB index from 24 to 15 hours. According to the Monetary Policy Report (2017Q2), the rationale for the adjustment is to avoid “repeated references to the daily movements of the USD exchange rate in the central parity of the following day” since the previous closing rate already incorporates such information to a large extent. However, this adjustment is widely believed to have had limited impact on the RMB exchange rate. The (overnight) 15-hour reference period starts at 4:30PM and ends at 7:30AM the next day, while the 24-hour reference period starts at 7:30AM and ends at 7:30AM the next day.15
To address the subtle timing issues, we use Bloomberg BFIX intraday data, which are available every 30 minutes on the hour and half-hour throughout the day. Based on the BFIX data, we can construct the index-implied dollar basketXt$X_{t}$and the spot rateStCL$S_{t}^{CL}$for all 48 half-hour intervals throughout the day.
The PBC confirmed on May 26, 2017 that it had modified the formation mechanism of the central parity rate by introducing the new countercyclical factor, although no detailed information has been disclosed about how the countercyclical factor is constructed.16The modification is believed to “give authorities more control over the fixing and restrain the influence of market pricing.”17The policy change is perceived by many market participants as a tool to address depreciation pressure without draining foreign reserves. However, it undermines earlier efforts to make the RMB more market-driven. The countercyclical factor was subsequently removed on January 9, 2018, reflecting the RMB's strength over the prior year as well as the dollar's protracted decline, reinstalled on August 24, 2018 against the backdrop of the U.S.-China trade war, and suspended again on October 26, 2020.
“To calculate the counter-cyclical factor, one begins by removing the impact of the currency basket from the movement between the previous closing rates and the central parity, after which the exchange-rate movements mainly reflect market supply and demand. The counter-cyclical factor can be found by adjusting the counter-cyclical coefficient, which is set by the quoting banks based on changes in the economic fundamentals and the extent of pro-cyclicality in the foreign-exchange market.”
We have shown so far that the formation mechanism of the central parity rate can be classified into one of the following three regimes: “Regime 1” with the 24-hour reference period, “Regime 2” with the 15-hour reference period without the countercyclical factor, and “Regime 3” with the 15-hour reference period together with the countercyclical factor. These three regimes are characterized by variants of the two-pillar policy in equations (1), (8), and (10), respectively.
We now empirically investigate whether the variants of the two-pillar policy hold in the data. To do so, we divide our full sample period—December 11, 2015 through December 31, 2020—into six subperiods that fall into one of the three regimes: Subperiod I (December 11, 2015 to February 19, 2017), Subperiod II (February 20, 2017 to May 25, 2017), Subperiod III (May 26, 2017 to January 8, 2018), Subperiod IV (January 9, 2018 to August 23, 2018), Subperiod V (August 24, 2018 to October 26, 2020), and Subperiod VI (October 27, 2020 to December 31, 2020). Specifically, Subperiod I falls in Regime 1, Subperiods II, IV, and VI fall in Regime 2, and Subperiods III and V fall in Regime 3.
We run regression (11) for Subperiod I, regression (12) for Subperiods II, IV, and VI, and regression (13) for Subperiods III and V. The results, reported in TableII, support our earlier results. Taking the CFETS index as an example (Panel A), theR2is fairly large and close to one, ranging from 0.97 to 0.99, in Subperiods IV and VI (Regime 2), and a bit smaller, at around 0.77 to 0.88, in Subperiods III and V (Regime 3). The results for the other two indices are similar.
Moreover, the regression coefficientβDT$\beta _{DT}$corresponds to the negative of the countercyclical coefficientwCCF$w_{CCF}$. From TableII, we can see that the countercyclical coefficient is estimated to be negative in the data. When it was introduced for the first time in Subperiod III, its magnitude in absolute value is relatively small, at around −0.13. As the U.S.-China trade war started and escalated in Subperiod V, the countercyclical factor was reintroduced. The countercyclical coefficient in this subperiod is larger in absolute value, at around −0.42, consistent with the PBC's goal of stabilizing the RMB against the backdrop of the trade war. Quantitatively, the regression results also provide an estimate of the unknown countercyclical coefficient of around 20%. During Subperiod V when the countercyclical factor was reinstalled, the weight of the market pillar declined from 50% of the overall weight to around 30% (i.e., 0.406/(0.406+0.620+0.423)=0.28).
We plot the regression residuals for the CFETS, SDR, and BIS indices in Figure5, Panels A, B, and C, respectively. Consider the results based on the CFETS index as an example (Panel A). We first see that the residuals are small overall, consistent with the largeR2reported in TablesIandII. For Subperiods IV and VI with anR2as high as 0.97 to 0.99, the residuals are negligible in magnitude, suggesting an almost perfect fit of our two-pillar policy in the data. We further see that the regression residuals in Subperiod I are generally negative, in the range of −20 to −40 basis points (bps) in late December 2015 and January 2016. The results suggest possible deliberate efforts of the PBC to keep the central parity rate strong against the backdrop of the Federal Reserve's liftoff decision in December 2015.
In Subperiods III and V with the countercyclical factor, the residuals exhibit negative jumps more frequently, suggesting an intent to keep the RMB strong. The frequency and intensity of the negative jumps are particularly prominent in Subperiod V when the U.S.-China trade war was in progress. In particular, as the trade war escalated, the RMB breached the level of 7 for the first time on August 5, 2019. Since then, the regression residuals hovered around −10 to −40 bps most of the time until mid-October when the United States and China reached the Phase 1 agreement. Occasionally, the residuals jump upward in Subperiod III with the countercyclical factor. In particular, on September 11 and 12, 2017, the residuals are 12 bps and 15 bps, respectively. The regression residuals are similar across different indices.
In the previous subsection, we estimate the correctly specified regressions for each of the six subperiods. In this subsection, we include the countercyclical factor in the regressions for all six periods as a falsification test to see whether the regression coefficient on the countercyclical factor is insignificantly different from zero for the subperiods with no such factor (i.e., Subperiods I, II, IV, and VI). Our results suggest that this is indeed the case: we can empirically reject the existence of the countercyclical factor in those subperiods without the factor.
We run regression (14) for all six subperiods. The results, reported in TableIII, strongly support the existence of the countercyclical factor in Subperiods III and V (Regime 3) in which the coefficientβCCF$\beta _{CCF}$is significantly negative and large in magnitude. Interestingly, the results generally support the nonexistence of the countercyclical factor in Subperiods II, IV, and VI (Regime 2). In fact, the coefficientβCCF$\beta _{CCF}$is generally insignificant in these subperiods, except in Subperiod IV when the coefficient is significant but small in magnitude. The results for Subperiod I (Regime 1) are also consistent with the 24-hour reference period, given that the coefficient estimates supportβNT≈−βCCF$\beta _{NT}\approx -\beta _{CCF}$.
As another robustness check, we also consider other possible explanatory variables such as the offshore exchange rate or the U.S. interest rate in addition to the two pillars. These variables may provide additional information about market supply and demand conditions. We find that including these additional variables makes little difference (see theInternet Appendix). For example, including the offshore exchange rate slightly reduces the coefficient on the market pillar but has little additional explanatory power beyond the two pillars.
Why has the two-pillar policy been chosen for China's recent exchange rate policy? We think that the choice is predicated on China's unique characteristics and is well suited for its transitional economy. Given the prominent role of international trade in China's economy, the basket pillar makes the value of the RMB stable with respect to the currencies of its major trade partners. In this regard, it is similar to Singapore's managed float against a (concealed) basket of currencies of its major trading partners and competitors (see, for example, Ong (2013)). However, unlike Singapore, China has one of the largest economies in the world with the RMB being one of the major reserve currencies. Moreover, its economy has become more consumption-oriented with an increasingly liberalized capital account. For these reasons, exchange rate flexibility via the market pillar is much needed for China to gain more monetary policy independence according to the Mundell-Fleming trilemma. In the next section, we develop a theoretical model to quantitatively assess the main trade-off between flexibility and stability.
article.II. Model:
In this section, we develop a conventional flexible-price monetary model for exchange rate determination based on Svensson (1994).18Our model provides a theoretical microfoundation for the two-pillar policy. We first discuss the model setup. We then estimate key model parameters using GMM. At the end of this section, we explore quantitative model implications.
There is an infinite number of periods with each period divided into two subperiods:AMandPM. In each period, the government in the home country (China) chooses the optimal central parity rate in subperiodAMand the optimal monetary and exchange rate policies in subperiodPM. Each period in the model corresponds to one day.
We now solve the government's problem for theAMperiod in equation (21) for the optimal formation mechanism for the central parity rate. The following proposition reports the result.
Proposition 1.Suppose that the value functionV(Xt)$\mathcal {V}(X_{t})$takes the quadratic form in (22). LettingVcc≡V(3,3)$ V_{cc}\equiv V^{(3,3) }$, the optimal central parity rate has the following two-pillar representation:



Proof.See AppendixB.1.□$\Box$

The optimal central parity rule in equation (23) resembles the two-pillar policy we have formulated. In particular, the first two terms correspond to the two pillars, resulting from the government's targets of maintaining exchange rate flexibility and current account stability. The third term results if the government also cares about the continuity of the central parity rate, which is negligible empirically. The last termht−1$h_{t-1}$represents the government's hedging demand as the state of the economy varies over time. We examine this term more closely later in this section.
Proposition 2.In a stationary rational expectations equilibrium, the optimal monetary policyut$u_{t}$solves the problem in (25) and is given by


Proof.See AppendixB.3.□$\Box$

Proposition2, together with Proposition1, fully characterizes the stationary equilibrium. In the discretionary equilibrium with the rational-expectations restrictiondt=DXt$d_{t}=DX_{t}$, the transition equation in (24) implies that the exchange rate deviationdt$d_{t}$linearly depends on both the state vectorXt$X_{t}$and the controlut$u_{t}$in equilibrium, that is,dt=HXt+Gut$d_{t}=HX_{t}+Gu_{t}$, as shown in (27). Under the optimal monetary policyut=FXt$u_{t}=FX_{t}$in equation (26), equation (27) imposes an explicit constraint onDas a result of rational expectations. We thus need to solve the matricesUandVin the value functions together withDjointly as a fixed point to the system of Bellman equations and the rational expectations restriction in equation (27).
We characterize the equilibrium in the benchmark case in which we set the target weightsξΔd$\xi _{\Delta d}$,ξΔi$\xi _{\Delta i}$, andξu$\xi _{u}$to zero. The following proposition summarizes the characterization of the equilibrium.
Proposition 3.Suppose thatξΔd=ξΔi=ξu=0$\xi _{\Delta d}=\xi _{\Delta i}=\xi _{u}=0$. Then the equilibrium is characterized as follows.



Proof.See AppendixC.1.□$\Box$

Importantly, the hedging termht−1$h_{t-1}$is small in magnitude. So, the optimal central parity rule in equation (23) is captured primarily by our two-pillar policy. In fact, when the government puts all weight ond2ori2, this term is zero. In the intermediate case, the magnitude of the hedging term is small. In addition, if the U.S. interest rate is independent over time, this term is zero because there is no longer demand for hedging foreign interest rate risk. Empirically, we find that the U.S. interest rate has little explanatory power.
Proposition3shows that in equilibrium, under the optimal monetary policy,ut=−qt−mt−1−F2it*−F6ct$u_{t}=-q_{t}-m_{t-1}-F_{2}i_{t}^{\ast }-F_{6}c_{t}$. In other words, the government optimally chooses the money supplymt=mt−1+ut$m_{t}=m_{t-1}+u_{t}$such that the exogenous real exchange rate shockqt$q_{t}$is fully absorbed. This explains why the hedging demandht−1$h_{t-1}$depends only onit−1*$i_{t-1}^{\ast }$, and not also onqt−1$q_{t-1}$. Intuitively, when the U.S. interest rate is independent over time (i.e.,ρi*=1/Δt$\rho _{i^{\ast }}=1/\Delta t$) or the government does not care about interest rate variability (i.e.,ξi=0$\xi _{i}=0$), demand for hedging foreign interest rate risk no longer exists, implying a zero hedging term.
In this subsection, we estimate key parameters of policy weights using GMM and calibrate the remaining parameters.
Specifically, we calibrate eight parameters: α, β,ρq$\rho _{q}$,ρi*$\rho _{i^{\ast }}$,ρΔx$\rho _{\Delta x}$,σq$\sigma _{q}$,σi*$\sigma _{i^{\ast }}$, andσΔx$\sigma _{\Delta x}$. Because the inflation data needed to construct the real exchange rate measure are available only at a monthly frequency, we focus on the monthly frequency in calibration and convert the daily data to monthly by keeping end-of-month observations.
Following Svensson (1994), the interest elasticity of the demand for money α is set to half a year, and the time discount factor β is set to 0.9913 for one month (or, equivalently, the annualized discount factor is equal to 0.9). To calibrate the rates of mean reversion for the real exchange rate, the U.S. interest rate, and the index-implied dollar basket (i.e.,ρq$\rho _{q}$,ρi*$\rho _{i^{\ast }}$, andρΔx$\rho _{\Delta x}$), we run univariate first-order autoregressions within the December 2015 to December 2020 sample period. We construct the real exchange rate measure using (17) based on the nominal exchange rate data as well as CPI inflation data for China and the United States For the index-implied dollar basket, we use the basket implied from the CFETS index for calibration; the results based on other RMB indices are similar.
The autoregression results suggest a large level of persistence inqt$q_{t}$andit*$ i_{t}^{\ast }$with AR(1) coefficients around 0.9 and 0.99, respectively. As a result, the calibrated values forρq$\rho _{q}$andρi*$\rho _{i^{\ast }}$are 1.48 and 0.10 per year. By contrast, the growth rate of the index-implied dollar indexΔxt$\Delta x_{t}$is serially uncorrelated with the AR(1) coefficient at nearly zero. We thus setρΔx=1/dt$\rho _{\Delta x}=1/dt$so that the processΔxt$\Delta x_{t}$is independent over time. From the autoregression results, we infer the values for the standard deviationsσq$\sigma _{q}$,σi*$\sigma _{i^{\ast }}$, andσΔx$ \sigma _{\Delta x}$. The calibrated parameter values are reported in TableIV.
We estimate the rest of the parameters using GMM. We focus on the benchmark case withξd=0$\xi _d=0$and setξc=1$\xi _c=1$as a normalization. Three parameters remain to estimate:ξi$\xi _{i}$,ξΔe$\xi _{\Delta e}$, andξΔx$\xi _{\Delta x}$. We estimate these parameters to match the following four sample moments: the variance of the Chinese interest rate,σ2(it)$\sigma ^2(i_{t})$, the variance of the (log) central parity rate,σ2(ct)$\sigma ^2(c_{t})$, the variance of the difference between the (log) central parity rate and the (log) market pillar,σ2(ct−et−1)$\sigma ^2(c_{t}-e_{t-1})$, and the variance of the difference between the (log) central parity rate and the (log) basket pillar,σ2(ct−s¯t)$\sigma ^2(c_{t}-\overline{s}_{t})$.
We conduct two-stage GMM estimation. In the first-stage GMM, we use the identity weighting matrix and thus weight all moments equally. Because the variance of the log central parity rate is 10 to 100 times larger than the other moments, the equal-weighting scheme leads to parameter estimates that match the interest rate variance as closely as possible. As a result, the point estimate forξΔe$\xi _{\Delta e}$(0.049) is about 10 times the estimate forξΔx$\xi _{\Delta x}$(0.005). The resulting pillar weights areω1=0.78$\omega _{1}=0.78$andω2=0.08$\omega _{2}=0.08$. That is, closely matching the central parity variance requires a disproportionately large weight on the market pillar.
In the second-stage GMM, we set the weighting matrix to be the inverse of the sample variance matrix of the moments. The point estimates, along with the Newey-West standard errors, are displayed in Panel A of TableV. The point estimate forξΔe$\xi _{\Delta e}$(0.070) is now only about 50% larger than the estimate forξΔx$\xi _{\Delta x}$(0.042). The resulting pillar weights are broadly in line with those in the data:ω1=0.48$\omega _{1}=0.48$andω2=0.29$\omega _{2}=0.29$. Furthermore, under the second-stage weighting matrix, the variances of the Chinese interest rate and the differences of central parity and the two pillars receive more weight. As a result, these moments are fitted more closely at the expense of the central parity variance, as shown in Panel B of TableV.
In this subsection, we explore the main model implications. We first provide a quantitative assessment of the flexibility-stability trade-off and show how a two-pillar policy is needed to balance key policy targets. We then demonstrate, both conceptually and quantitatively, that the countercyclical factor can arise endogenously in the model when the government aims to stabilize changes in the exchange rate deviation. Lastly, we discuss how sentiment-induced exchange rate volatility can destabilize the market and how effectively direct government interventions can counteract the irrational factor as an alternative to the approach of using the countercyclical factor.
The estimation results allow us to quantitatively illustrate the trade-off between flexibility and stability that the government faces. For this purpose, we consider counterfactuals where we vary the target weightsξΔe$\xi _{\Delta e}$andξΔx$\xi _{\Delta x}$to shut down one pillar at a time.
TableVIreports the results from the counterfactual analysis. As shown in Panel A, the interest rate's standard deviation in the data is 0.85%. The standard deviation of the exchange rate deviation from the central parity rate is 0.29%. The standard deviation of the difference between the central parity rate and the previous closing rate (i.e.,ct−et−1$c_{t}-e_{t-1}$) is 0.20%, while the standard deviation of the difference between the central parity rate and the basket pillar (i.e.,ct−s¯t$c_{t}-\overline{s}_{t}$) is smaller at 0.19%. Finally, the central parity rate's standard deviation is 3.15, and the monthly money growth rate's standard deviation is 0.73%.
Panel B of the table reports the model-implied moments based on the estimated parameter values. The results show that the pillar weights are closer to each other, withω1=0.48$\omega _{1}=0.48$andω2=0.29$\omega _{2}=0.29$. The next four moments are the same as the target moments reported in Panel B of TableV, but in terms of standard deviations. The last two columns also report two nontarget moments: the standard deviations of the exchange rate deviation and the (monthly) money growth rate. Compared to the data, the model does a reasonably good job of matching these nontarget moments, although it implies a less volatile exchange rate deviation and a more volatile money growth rate relative to the counterparts in the data.
First, we consider the case in which the government does not care about current account variability, which implies zero weight on basket stability (i.e.,ξΔx=0$\xi _{\Delta x}=0$andξΔe=0.0702$\xi _{\Delta e}=0.0702$). The results are reported in Panel C. In this case, the central parity rate has a single pillar, namely, the market pillar. As a result, the central parity rate is almost equal to the previous close with the weightw1close to one andw2=0$w_2=0$. The resulting standard deviation ofct−et−1$c_{t}-e_{t-1}$is close to that in the data. However, the difference between the central parity rate and the basket pillar is more than 40% more volatile than in the data. Put differently, by shifting all of the weight from the basket pillar to the market pillar, the government makes the central parity rate more market driven, but at the expense of a more volatile current account.
Next, we consider the other polar case in which the government cares exclusively about stabilizing the current account, which implies shifting the weight completely fromξΔe$\xi _{\Delta e}$toξΔx$\xi _{\Delta x}$(i.e.,ξΔx=0.0422$\xi _{\Delta x} = 0.0422$andξΔe=0$\xi _{\Delta e} = 0$). The results are reported in Panel D. Here, the government allows more volatility in the central parity rate, the spot exchange rate, and the domestic interest rate caused by foreign exchange shocks arising from the basket currencies. The results in this case indicate higher volatility in the exchange rate deviationdt$d_{t}$or the difference between the central parity rate and the market pillar. For example,σ(ct−et−1)$\sigma (c_{t}-e_{t-1})$is now equal to 12.62%, compared to 0.19% in the previous case. However, at the expense of higher levels of volatility in these targets, the domestic government achieves a more stable current account with volatility now only 0.10% versus 0.27% in the previous case.
Comparing the counterfactual results in Panels C and D with those in Panel B based on the estimated parameter values when the government puts significant weight on both pillars (i.e.,ξΔe=0.0702$\xi _{\Delta e}=0.0702$andξΔx=0.0422$\xi _{\Delta x} = 0.0422$), we see that the major advantage of having a two-pillar policy is to balance the targets with respect to both pillars. Unlike in cases in Panels B and C, the volatility levels of both targets are now balanced and closely match those in the data.
In this subsection, we show that the countercyclical factor can arise endogenously when the government aims to stabilize changes in the exchange rate deviation. Our model provides a rationale for the countercyclical factor when it is associated with the policy targetξΔd(dt−dt−1)2$\xi _{\Delta d}(d_{t}-d_{t-1})^2$.
Empirically, in the data, changes in the exchange rate deviation appear to be less volatile in subperiods when the countercyclical factor is implemented. Specifically, we find that in the data, the standard deviation of changes in the exchange rate deviation,σ(Δdt)$\sigma (\Delta d_{t})$, is 0.25% in Subperiod III when the countercyclical factor is introduced for the first time, which is lower than the standard deviation of 0.27% in the prior subperiods. The standard deviation of changes in the exchange rate deviation then increases to 0.39% in Subperiod IV when the countercyclical factor is suspended and decreases to 0.34% in Subperiod V when the counterfactual factor is reinstalled. Variance ratio tests further show that a decrease (increase) in the standard deviation is generally significant when the countercyclical factor is imposed (removed).
Theoretically, we show that the optimal central parity rule does indeed include the countercyclical factor in the presence of the policy targetξΔd(dt−dt−1)2$\xi _{\Delta d}(d_{t}-d_{t-1})^2$. To simplify the analysis, we setξΔe=ξΔx=0$\xi _{\Delta e}=\xi _{\Delta x}=0$so that the weightsw1,w2, andw3are all zero and the central parity rate is fully determined by the hedging termht−1$h_{t-1}$instead.
Proposition 4.SupposeξΔd>0$\xi _{\Delta d}>0$andξΔe=ξΔx=0$\xi _{\Delta e}=\xi _{\Delta x}=0$. Then, in equilibrium, there is an additional adjustment termg·dt−1$g \cdot d_{t-1}$in the optimal central parity rule such that the coefficientgis negative and approximately proportional toξΔd/Δt$\xi _{\Delta d}/\Delta t$.


Proof.See AppendixC.2.□$\Box$

Proposition4provides a rationale for the countercyclical factor. Importantly, the adjustment termg·dt−1$g \cdot d_{t-1}$in the proposition corresponds to the (log) countercyclical factorCCFt−1$CCF_{t-1}$formulated in equation (9) in our empirical analysis. In our model, the basket-implied dollar index remains unchanged during the daytime and then changes byΔxt$\Delta x_{t}$overnight. So, the daytime component of the basket pillar,S¯t−1DT$\overline{S}_{t-1}^{DT}$, is equal toSt−1CP$S_{t-1}^{CP}$. As a result, the logarithm of the countercyclical factor,log(CCFt−1)=wCCF·dt−1$\text{log}(CCF_{t-1})=w_{CCF} \cdot d_{t-1}$, is equal to the adjustment termg·dt−1$g \cdot d_{t-1}$, with the negative coefficientgcorresponding to the countercyclical coefficientwCCF$w_{CCF}$. In line with our discussion under equation (10), the negative countercyclical coefficientgessentially reduces the weight on the market pillar and thus increases the weight on the basket pillar.21
In practice, the countercyclical factor is used to counteract the “irrational expectations” of foreign exchange investors according to the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2017Q2). In other words, suppose that irrational trading leads to an unusually large depreciation of the spot rate, resulting in a large deviationdt−1$d_{t-1}$. By including the termg·dt−1$g \cdot d_{t-1}$in the central parity rule, the government in the model boosts the RMB by reducing the central parity rate, which dampens expectations of further depreciation (see equation (B.1)). In this way, the government stabilizes the exchange rate deviation.
To quantitatively assess the implication above about the countercyclical factor, we consider another counterfactual by imposing an additional targetξΔd(dt−dt−1)2$\xi _{\Delta d}(d_{t}-d_{t-1})^2$in the government's objective function. Specifically, we set the target weight toξΔd=0.01$\xi _{\Delta d}=0.01$. We report the results in Panel E of TableVI. The results suggest that relative to the benchmark case in Panel B, imposing this additional target reduces the standard deviations of both the exchange rate deviation and its changes. Furthermore, we find that the coefficientgin Proposition4is equal to −0.06. Taking the impact of the additional target into account essentially decreases the weight of the market pillar from 0.44 to 0.40 and increases the weight of the basket pillar from 0.26 to 0.28 (see footnote 20).
In summary, the introduction of the countercyclical factor is consistent with imposing an additional targetξΔd(dt−dt−1)2$\xi _{\Delta d}(d_{t}-d_{t-1})^2$in the government's objective function. In the next subsection, we consider direct government intervention, an alternative tool to combat irrationality in the foreign exchange market.
As stated in the PBC's Monetary Policy Report (2017Q2), the countercyclical factor is used to counteract “irrational” behavior or a “herding effect” in the foreign exchange market. To shed light on the rationale behind the countercyclical factor, we extend the baseline model to account for possible irrational noise trading (De Long, Shleifer, Summers, and Waldmann (1990)). Details about the extended model and the results can be found in theInternet Appendix. We discuss the main findings here.
First, we show that in the presence of noise trading, exchange rate volatility depends on both fundamentals and noise. An exogenous increase in the amount of noise trading has a direct positive impact on exchange rate volatility but also an indirect positive impact through the risk premium channel. Facing more noise trading leads investors to demand a higher risk premium for providing liquidity to noise traders, which then feeds back into higher exchange rate volatility. Due to the feedback loop, there are two distinct values for exchange rate volatility in equilibrium, reminiscent of the findings in Jeanne and Rose (2002) and Brunnermeier, Sockin, and Xiong (2020). Following the latter, we assume that the lower exchange rate volatility is realized and thegoodequilibrium results.
Second, similar to Brunnermeier, Sockin, and Xiong (2020), we show that if too much noise trading is in the market, there may not exist any risk premium that could induce investors to take on any position, resulting in a market breakdown. Such destabilizing effects of irrational noise trading may explain the motive behind the PBC's introduction of the countercyclical factor.
Lastly, we argue that direct (intraday) government intervention may be a direct, and possibly more effective, way to counteract the irrational factor in the foreign exchange market. In fact, we show that through the “leaning-against-the-noise” channel, direct government intervention helps reduce exchange rate volatility (see Brunnermeier, Sockin, and Xiong (2020) for leaning-against-the-noise interventions in stock markets). If the government could intervene directly, part of the effect of noise trading shocks could be offset, resulting in lower exchange rate volatility and less likelihood of market failure. In addition, direct government intervention not only is more direct and more effective in mitigating the destabilizing effects of irrational noise trading, but it also avoids a main drawback of the approach of using the countercyclical factor—the latter approach makes the central parity formation mechanism less market oriented and may hurt the government's credibility. Of course, direct government intervention could be costly in terms of a negative impact on foreign reserves.
article.III. Conclusions:
China's exchange rate policy affects not only the Chinese economy but also global financial markets, and thus, it is important to understand China's exchange rate policy. Our paper is the first academic study to provide an in-depth analysis of China's recent two-pillar policy for the RMB. We provide empirical evidence consistent with a two-pillar policy that aims to achieve a balance between exchange rate flexibility and stability against an RMB index.
In light of the empirical evidence of a two-pillar policy, we quantitatively evaluate China's exchange rate policy using a flexible-price monetary model of the RMB that we develop in this paper. The theoretical model features policy trade-offs between the variances of the exchange rate, the interest rate, and the current account. We show that the two-pillar policy arises endogenously as an optimal solution to the government's problem of minimizing the variances in exchange rate deviations and the current account. We further extend the model to understand the rationale behind intraday government interventions that have been shown to be an effective tool for “leaning against noise traders” in the presence of noise-trading risk.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
article.Appendix A: Data:
The main data sources are CFETS and Bloomberg.
From the CFETS website (http://www.chinamoney.com.cn), we retrieve historical data related to central parity rates and the RMB indices.
From Bloomberg, we obtain daily data on spot exchange rates, three-month SHIBOR and LIBOR interest rates, as well as monthly data on China's M2 money supply, CPI, and foreign reserves. In addition, we obtain intraday exchange rate data from the Bloomberg BFIX, which are available every 30 minutes on the hour and half-hour throughout the day. For each week, the BFIX data begin on Sunday 5:30PM New York time and end on Friday 5PM New York time. We then use the BFIX data to construct intraday values for the U.S. dollar (DXY), CFETS, and SDR indices. For a given index, we collect BFIX data for all constituent currencies and then convert the data to China local time, taking into account time-zone differences and daylight savings periods. Based on the BFIX data, we can construct the index-implied dollar basket and the RMB spot rate for all 48 half-hour intervals throughout the day.
article.Appendix B: Derivation of the Model:
In this appendix, we provide a sketch of the derivation of the general model. Propositions1and2are proved as part of the derivation. A more detailed derivation is presented in theInternet Appendix.
We decompose the state vectorXt$X_{t}$into the vector of exogenous shocksXt(1)=(qt,it*)′$X_{t}^{(1) }=(q_{t},i_{t}^{\ast }) ^{\prime }$, predetermined variablesXt(2)=(mt−1,dt−1,it−1)′$X_{t}^{(2) }=(m_{t-1},d_{t-1},i_{t-1}) ^{\prime }$, and the endogenous variableXt(3)=ct$X_{t}^{(3) }=c_{t}$. The coefficient matrixVin the value functionV(Xt)$\mathcal {V}(X_{t})$is also decomposed accordingly. Similarly, we decompose the state vectorYt$Y_{t}$intoYt(1)=(Δxt,ct−1)′$Y_{t}^{(1) }=(\Delta x_{t},c_{t-1}) ^{\prime }$,Yt(2)=(qt−1,it−1*)′$Y_{t}^{(2) }=(q_{t-1},i_{t-1}^{\ast }) ^{\prime }$, andYt(3)=(mt−1,dt−1,it−1)′$Y_{t}^{(3) }=(m_{t-1},d_{t-1},i_{t-1}) ^{\prime }$, and we similarly decomposeU.
article.Appendix C: Benchmark and Special Cases:
In this appendix, we derive the benchmark case as well as a few special cases. Detailed proofs are in theInternet Appendix.
In the benchmark case, we setξΔd=0$\protect \xi _{\Delta d}=0$,ξΔi=0$\protect \xi _{\Delta i}=0$,ξu=0$\protect \xi _{u}=0$, andξΔc=0$\protect \xi _{\Delta c}=0$. In this case, we show that the optimal central parity rule is almost the same as the two-pillar policy, except thatht−1=h·it−1*$h_{t-1}=h \cdot i^{\ast }_{t-1}$.
As derived in theInternet Appendix, we can further show thatD=0D2000D6$D= \def\eqcellsep{&}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & D_{2} & 0 & 0 & 0 & D_{6}  \end{bmatrix}$,F*=1F2100F6$F^{\ast }= \def\eqcellsep{&}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & F_{2} & 1 & 0 & 0 & F_{6}  \end{bmatrix}$,H=GH2G00H6$H= \def\eqcellsep{&}\begin{bmatrix} G & H_{2} & G & 0 & 0 & H_{6}  \end{bmatrix}$, andG=Δt/α1+Δt/α−w1(1+D6)$G=\frac{\Delta t/\alpha }{1+\Delta t/\alpha -w_{1}(1+D_{6}) }$, whereD2andD6are the second and sixth elements ofD, respectively, andF2,F6,H2, andH6are similarly defined. These elements are determined endogenously together with the matrixV. After tedious algebra (see theInternet Appendixfor detailed derivations), we can indeed verify that the matrixVtakes the conjectured form.
To deriveg, we need to deriveV64andV66(i.e.,Vcc$V_{cc}$) based on equation (28). The derivation in this special case is much more complicated than that for the benchmark case. For tractability, we derive the first-order approximation of the solution whenξΔd$\xi _{\Delta d}$is sufficiently small.
